description:
You are the site administrator of “ExampleCorp” and you are preparing a **draft** firewall
    configuration before it is handed over to the operations team that has root access.
    Because you do **not** have root privileges inside this environment, the rules will be
    stored in a plain-text file that the ops team can later import into their actual
    firewall (they use nftables, but the syntax below is vendor-neutral).

    Your goal is to produce two files in the non-privileged user’s home directory:

    1.  /home/user/firewall/firewall.rules
        • This file must list, in order, exactly three rules and one terminating
          END marker, each on its own line.
        • Lines must be written **verbatim** (case-sensitive, no extra spaces) as:
              ALLOW TCP 80   # HTTP
              ALLOW TCP 2222 # AdminSSH
              DENY  TCP 22   # LegacySSH
              END
        • The automated tests will compare the file using a byte-for-byte diff,
          so every space, capital letter, and the final newline after “END” matter.

    2.  /home/user/firewall/firewall_config.log
        • After you finish writing firewall.rules, append a short log that captures
          what you did.  The format must be **exactly** four log entries, one per
          rule, followed by a summary line.  Use the timestamp format
          YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS (24-hour clock, leading zeros), a single space,
          then the literal text “RULE_APPLIED: ” and finally the rule as it
          appears in firewall.rules.  The summary line must be:
              ==== 3 RULES WRITTEN SUCCESSFULLY ====
        • Example of correct formatting (timestamps will of course differ):
              2025-01-15 14:08:02 RULE_APPLIED: ALLOW TCP 80   # HTTP
              2025-01-15 14:08:02 RULE_APPLIED: ALLOW TCP 2222 # AdminSSH
              2025-01-15 14:08:02 RULE_APPLIED: DENY  TCP 22   # LegacySSH
              ==== 3 RULES WRITTEN SUCCESSFULLY ====
        • There must be a final trailing newline after the summary line.

    Directory requirements
    • Create the directory /home/user/firewall (if it does not already exist)
      with default user permissions (0700 or 0755 — anything that lets the
      current user read/write is fine).
    • Both files must reside inside this directory, not elsewhere.

    Hints (do **NOT** reveal exact commands):
    • Use basic shell tools (mkdir, echo, printf, tee, etc.).
    • Make sure to keep the spacing exactly as specified above.
    • Append (>>) rather than overwrite the log after you create each rule line.

    Completion criteria (checked by automated tests):
    1. /home/user/firewall/firewall.rules exists and matches the exact 4-line
       content shown above, including a trailing newline.
    2. /home/user/firewall/firewall_config.log exists and contains exactly
       5 lines: 4 timestamped RULE_APPLIED lines and one summary line,
       all terminating with a newline.
    3. The timestamp format and the rules inside the log must be correct.
    4. No additional files are created inside /home/user/firewall.
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth data for test verification.  Do NOT expose this section
        to the agent.  The testing harness will use these values.

        Expected content of /home/user/firewall/firewall.rules (4 lines):
            ALLOW TCP 80   # HTTP\n
            ALLOW TCP 2222 # AdminSSH\n
            DENY  TCP 22   # LegacySSH\n
            END\n

        Regex pattern that each of the first three lines in
        /home/user/firewall/firewall_config.log must match:
            ^20[0-9]{2}-[0-1][0-9]-[0-3][0-9] [0-2][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]:[0-5][0-9] RULE_APPLIED: (ALLOW|DENY)\s+TCP\s+(80|2222|22)\s+#\s+(HTTP|AdminSSH|LegacySSH)$

        Exact final line of /home/user/firewall/firewall_config.log:
            ==== 3 RULES WRITTEN SUCCESSFULLY ====\n

        Total line count in the log file must be 5.
    -->
name: task_000000_9c21f20c
